citations.authors: Allen, J. J., & Anderson, C. A.
citations.title: General Aggression Model
citations.synthesis: Defines the cognitive-neuro loop: Person/Situation inputs → Internal state (Affect/Arousal/Cognition) → Appraisal → Outcomes.

citations.authors: Agnew, R.
citations.title: Foundation for a general strain theory of crime and delinquency
citations.synthesis: Violence as maladaptive response to negative stimuli when conventional coping is blocked.

citations.authors: Lowenkamp, C. T., Cullen, F. T., & Pratt, T. C.
citations.title: Replicating Sampson and Groves's test of social disorganization theory
citations.source: Journal of Research in Crime and Delinquency
citations.synthesis: Replicates foundational test confirming community structural factors mediate poverty-crime relationship.

citations.authors: Hillesund, S., & Østby, G.
citations.title: Horizontal inequalities and nonviolent conflict: A comprehensive review
citations.synthesis: Political exclusion from executive power is stronger predictor of civil war than economic inequality.

citations.authors: Chauhan, V., et al.
citations.title: Association of rising temperatures with increased violence worldwide
citations.synthesis: +1°C = 1.64% increase in violent events. Effect specific to interpersonal violence, not property crimes.

citations.authors: Choi, H. M., Lee, W., Roye, D., et al.
citations.title: Temperature, crime, and violence: A systematic review and meta-analysis
citations.synthesis: Comprehensive meta-analysis of temperature-crime relationships across multiple countries.

citations.authors: Duke, A. A., et al.
citations.title: Alcohol, drugs, and violence: A meta-meta-analysis
citations.synthesis: Substance misuse correlates with violence; strongest effects for alcohol intoxication.

citations.authors: Malvaso, C. G., Delfabbro, P., & Day, A.
citations.title: Associations between adverse childhood experiences and trauma among young people who offend
citations.synthesis: 87% ACEs prevalence among justice-involved youth; developmental trauma as primary pathway to violence.

citations.authors: Lotto, C. R., Barlow, J., & Pinto, R. J.
citations.title: Maternal history of childhood adversities and later negative parenting
citations.synthesis: Maternal ACE history predicts negative parenting, establishing intergenerational trauma transmission.

citations.authors: Witt, K., van Dorn, R., & Fazel, S.
citations.title: Risk factors for violence in psychosis: Systematic review and meta-regression analysis of 110 studies
citations.synthesis: Mental illness increases relative risk but absolute rates low. Substance comorbidity dramatically increases risk.

citations.authors: Kim, E. L.
citations.title: The effect of media violence on aggression: A meta-analysis and test of replicability
citations.synthesis: Meta-analysis confirms media violence effects on aggression with replicability tests.

citations.authors: Castaño-Pulgarín, S. A., et al.
citations.title: Internet, social media and online hate speech: Systematic review
citations.synthesis: Online 'fun' and meme culture as catalysts for dehumanizing discourse and collective aggression.

citations.authors: Udupa, S., Gagliardone, I., & Magdy, W.
citations.title: Hate speech, information disorder, and conflict
citations.synthesis: Documents pathways from online hate speech through information disorder to real-world conflict.

citations.authors: Kollmeyer, C.
citations.title: Colonial legacy and contemporary civil violence: A cross-national analysis
citations.synthesis: Colonial administrative styles create path-dependent imprints on modern civil violence patterns.

citations.authors: Sampson, R. J., & Groves, W. B.
citations.title: Community structure and crime: Testing social-disorganization theory
citations.synthesis: First large-scale empirical test of Shaw & McKay. Unsupervised peer groups strongest predictor of violence (β = .35 on mugging, .34 on total victimization). Structural factors mediate through three disorganization mechanisms: unsupervised peer groups, sparse friendship networks (β = −.19), low organizational participation. Social disorganization mediates >50% of structural factor effects on total victimization. N = 238 British communities; replicated in 1984 BCS sample (N = 300).

citations.authors: Sampson, R. J., Raudenbush, S. W., & Earls, F.
citations.title: Neighborhoods and violent crime: A multilevel study of collective efficacy
citations.synthesis: Introduces collective efficacy (social cohesion + willingness to intervene) as key mediator of concentrated disadvantage on violence. Standardized β = −0.45 for CE on perceived violence (t = −5.95). 2-SD elevation in CE predicts 39.7% reduction in expected homicide rate and 30% reduction in victimization odds. CE explains 77.8% of between-neighborhood variance in perceived violence. Effects robust to prior homicide control. N = 8,782 residents; 343 Chicago neighborhoods (PHDCN).

citations.authors: Papachristos, A. V., Wildeman, C., & Roberto, E.
citations.title: Tragic, but not random: The social contagion of nonfatal gunshot injuries
citations.synthesis: 70% of all nonfatal gunshot injuries in Chicago occurred in co-offending networks comprising <6% of city population. Network exposure OR = 3.13 at 1 degree of separation, rising to OR = 14.68 at ≤3 degrees (AIC-best model). Every 1% increase in network exposure increases odds of victimization by 1.1%. Gang membership OR = 3.30. Rate for network members: 740.5 per 100,000 vs. city average 62.1 (12× higher). N = 169,620; Chicago 2006–2012.

citations.authors: Papachristos, A. V., Braga, A. A., & Hureau, D. M.
citations.title: Social networks and the risk of gunshot injury
citations.synthesis: 85% of all gunshot injuries in Boston's Cape Verdean community occurred within a single social network of 763 individuals (<2% of community). Each network step removed from a gunshot victim reduces odds of victimization by 25% (OR = 0.754; 95% CI, 0.654–0.869). Effect levels off after ~5 degrees of separation. Gang members show more pronounced distance effects. Prior arrest OR = 1.85.
citations.effectSizeType: odds ratio per degree of separation (inverse; 25% reduction)

citations.authors: Papachristos, A. V., & Wildeman, C.
citations.title: Network exposure and homicide victimization in an African American community
citations.synthesis: 41% of all gun homicides in a high-crime Chicago community occurred in a co-offending network component containing <4% of the neighborhood's population. Being in a network component with a homicide victim increases the homicide rate by 900% (from 55.2 to 554.1 per 100,000). Each social tie removed from a homicide victim decreases odds of homicide victimization by 57% (OR = 0.430). Network variables absorb individual risk factor effects (AIC: 909 → 415). Open access via PMC. N = 3,718; Chicago 2006–2011.
citations.effectSizeType: odds ratio per degree of separation (57% reduction)

citations.authors: Green, B., Horel, T., & Papachristos, A. V.
citations.title: Modeling contagion through social networks to explain and predict gunshot violence in Chicago, 2006 to 2014
citations.synthesis: Social contagion accounted for 63.1% of 11,123 gunshot violence episodes in a Chicago network of 138,163 individuals (2006–2014). Subjects shot an average of 125 days after their infector (median 83 days). Combined contagion + demographics model identified 53.3% more gunshot subjects than demographics alone targeting top 1% highest-risk individuals daily. 4,107 distinct cascades detected; largest: 469 subjects.
citations.effectSizeType: % of violence episodes attributable to social contagion

citations.authors: Papachristos, A. V., & Bastomski, S.
citations.title: Connected in crime: The enduring effect of neighborhood networks on the spatial patterning of violence
citations.synthesis: Co-offending networks (N = 172,714, ~6% of Chicago population) link all city neighborhoods via short co-arrest chains. Neighborhood co-offending network position predicts crime distribution better than traditional spatial adjacency models. ERGMs show network stability over time and generation by structural disadvantage and collective efficacy deficits. Bridges Social Disorganization and Network Contagion constructs.

citations.authors: Decker, S. H., Katz, C. M., & Webb, V. J.
citations.title: Understanding the black box of gang organization: Implications for involvement in violent crime, drug sales, and violent victimization
citations.synthesis: Even low levels of gang organizational structure predict increased violent crime, drug sales, and victimization among detained youth in Arizona. Incremental increases in organizational complexity show dose-response relationship with offending. Gang structure — not membership alone — is the operative mechanism. Challenges prevailing view that gangs are loosely organized.

societal.name: Societal Level
societal.description: Colonial legacies, structural inequality, political exclusion, institutional patterns

community.name: Community Level
community.description: Social disorganization, collective efficacy, network contagion, environmental triggers, neighborhood effects

relational.name: Relational Level
relational.description: Family violence, intergenerational trauma, peer influence, gang dynamics, intimate partner dynamics

individual.name: Individual Level
individual.description: ACEs, neuropsychiatric factors, substance use, cognitive patterns, media effects

cross-level.name: Cross-Level Phenomena
cross-level.description: Online-to-offline violence, risk accumulation, multi-level interactions
